categoryName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms

questions.topicName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms
questions.stem: This low- and high-power histology of a well-demarcated parotid tumor shows a mixture of ductal (epithelial) and myoepithelial cells set within a distinctive background matrix. Which matrix is characteristic of this tumor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dense lymphoid stroma with germinal centers

explanation.summary: Pleomorphic adenoma shows epithelial and myoepithelial cells embedded in a chondromyxoid matrix. This mixed pattern distinguishes it from Warthin tumor (lymphoid stroma) and mucoepidermoid carcinoma (mucous cells).
explanation.whyCorrect: Pleomorphic adenoma shows epithelial and myoepithelial cells embedded in a chondromyxoid matrix. This mixed pattern distinguishes it from Warthin tumor (lymphoid stroma) and mucoepidermoid carcinoma (mucous cells).

questions.topicName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms
questions.stem: This salivary gland tumor shows a double layer of oncocytic epithelial cells resting on a dense lymphoid stroma that contains a follicular germinal center. Which tumor and risk-factor association is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warthin tumor - strongly associated with smoking

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adenoid cystic carcinoma - associated with perineural invasion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma - associated with a chromosomal translocation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pleomorphic adenoma - associated with prior radiation exposure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic sialadenitis - associated with ductal obstruction

explanation.summary: A bilayered oncocytic epithelium over lymphoid stroma with germinal centers is diagnostic of Warthin tumor. It arises almost exclusively in the parotid gland and is strongly linked to smoking.
explanation.whyCorrect: A bilayered oncocytic epithelium over lymphoid stroma with germinal centers is diagnostic of Warthin tumor. It arises almost exclusively in the parotid gland and is strongly linked to smoking.

questions.topicName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms
questions.stem: This contrast-enhanced axial neck CT shows an infiltrating soft-tissue mass with a small adjacent regional node, with a coronal reconstruction confirming its location. In the workup of an oral cavity neoplasm, what is the primary purpose of obtaining this study?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To establish the definitive tissue diagnosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: To measure salivary gland flow rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: To assess the extent of locoregional and metastatic disease (staging)

explanation.summary: CT of the neck and chest with contrast is used to stage head and neck cancer by defining the primary tumor extent and nodal/metastatic spread; a definitive diagnosis still requires tissue sampling.
explanation.whyCorrect: CT of the neck and chest with contrast is used to stage head and neck cancer by defining the primary tumor extent and nodal/metastatic spread; a definitive diagnosis still requires tissue sampling.

questions.topicName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms
questions.stem: The clinical photograph shows an ulcerated, indurated lesion of the oral mucosa, and the corresponding histology shows nests and islands of malignant keratinocytes invading the underlying stroma. Which risk-factor combination is most classically associated with this lesion?

explanation.summary: This is keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity, classically driven by combined tobacco and alcohol exposure, in contrast to HPV-driven nonkeratinizing oropharyngeal SCC.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity, classically driven by combined tobacco and alcohol exposure, in contrast to HPV-driven nonkeratinizing oropharyngeal SCC.

questions.topicName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms
questions.stem: This schematic maps the two main esophageal cancers to their typical anatomic locations. The lesion depicted in the distal third of the esophagus most commonly arises from which precursor lesion?

explanation.summary: The distal-third tumor is adenocarcinoma, which arises from Barrett esophagus (intestinal metaplasia) due to chronic acid reflux. Squamous cell carcinoma predominates in the upper two-thirds.
explanation.whyCorrect: The distal-third tumor is adenocarcinoma, which arises from Barrett esophagus (intestinal metaplasia) due to chronic acid reflux. Squamous cell carcinoma predominates in the upper two-thirds.

questions.topicName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms
questions.stem: These two gross esophageal specimens show malignant lesions: one (A) extending into the gastric cardia distally and the other (B) producing a stricture in the mid-esophagus. Which risk factor is most specific to the distal lesion in panel A?

explanation.summary: The distal lesion extending into the gastric cardia (A) is adenocarcinoma, driven by chronic GERD and Barrett esophagus. The mid-esophageal stricturing lesion (B) is squamous cell carcinoma, linked to smoking, alcohol, and caustic injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: The distal lesion extending into the gastric cardia (A) is adenocarcinoma, driven by chronic GERD and Barrett esophagus. The mid-esophageal stricturing lesion (B) is squamous cell carcinoma, linked to smoking, alcohol, and caustic injury.

questions.topicName: Oral, Salivary Gland, and Esophageal Neoplasms
questions.stem: This esophageal biopsy shows malignant infiltrating nests of squamous epithelium with no background of Barrett metaplasia. Which cancer, and its strongest risk factor, does this represent?

explanation.summary: Nests of malignant squamous epithelium without Barrett metaplasia indicate squamous cell carcinoma, most strongly associated with smoking (also alcohol, hot liquids, and caustic ingestion); it favors the upper two-thirds of the esophagus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nests of malignant squamous epithelium without Barrett metaplasia indicate squamous cell carcinoma, most strongly associated with smoking (also alcohol, hot liquids, and caustic ingestion); it favors the upper two-thirds of the esophagus.
